name: Noss Goes to the Lair
description: Investigator Noss has concluded that the missing soldiers were taken by the fresh Hollow, who left a memorial formation as a message. She needs to go into the abomination lair and ask directly. She wants an escort who's been there before.
accept_text: She has already packed what she needs: her notes, her sketches of the track pattern, the measurement records. She has not packed a weapon. 'I don't take weapons into a negotiation,' she says, which is the first time she's used the word negotiation for what she's doing, but it clearly fits better than investigation. 'The Hollow haven't been hostile to non-threatening individuals in three hundred years of documented interaction. The fresh one is communicative.' She looks south, toward the lair. 'I have three questions. First: where are the three soldiers. Second: why were they taken and under what conditions are they being held. Third: what was the memorial formation meant to communicate.' She is quiet. 'I know the third answer already, I think. It was meant to communicate: we have your people and we did not harm them and we want you to know this. The formation was reassurance.' She pauses. 'I want to confirm that reading and understand why reassurance was the first message.' She looks at you. 'You've spoken to the fresh one. It responds to you. Come with me. Don't speak unless I ask you to — I want to conduct this as formally as I conduct everything, which means I ask the questions in the sequence I've prepared, I record the responses, and I leave with a complete account.' She pauses. 'And if the answer to where are the soldiers is: they're here, and they're whole, and they stayed voluntarily — I want to be ready for that too.'
complete_text: The fresh Hollow vassal listens to Noss's questions with the quality of something that has been waiting for exactly this. Not impatiently — with the stillness of something that understood what the memorial formation was for and has been waiting to see if anyone read it correctly. The three soldiers are here. They are whole. When Noss asks the second question — under what conditions — the vassal takes time to answer, which you've learned means the answer is in a form that requires effort to translate. What comes through: the soldiers came to the perimeter. The Abominations registered them as threat. The vassal intercepted before the response. The soldiers were brought inside the perimeter to the interior, where the Abominations don't go, and have been here since. They have not been harmed. They have been — observed. The vassal wanted to understand them. It has not known how to say: the perimeter is protection, not prison. The memorial formation was the closest available language. Noss records all of this with the precise unhurried attention of someone who has spent their career documenting what people mean rather than what they say. When she finishes, she is quiet for a long moment. Then: 'Tell it — them — the soldiers want to go back. That is not a judgment. They have people waiting for them.' The vassal understands. The three soldiers, who have been in the interior and who are whole and who have spent the time in a way that shows on their faces, come forward. Noss writes something in her notes: the perimeter is not a boundary of aggression. It is the edge of a community that does not know how to say: come in, if you want to.
